metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit test scenarios for `batch_write_and_push` in `tests/unit/test_store_batch_write.py`: (a) happy-path verifying commit occurs and push is attempted, (b) push-failure is swallowed (best-effort contract), and (c) returned event count matches input.
findings.finding: `batch_write_and_push` is an explicit AC item (a new user-facing function) but does not appear in any named test scenario in the test plan — neither its happy path (commit+push sequence) nor its push-failure/best-effort-swallow behavior is listed.
findings.location: AC item 2 (`batch_write_and_push` variant) and AC item 7 (test scenarios list in `tests/unit/test_store_batch_write.py`)
findings.suggested_fix: Add two scenarios to AC item 7: `batch_write_and_push happy-path: commit precedes push attempt` and `batch_write_and_push push-failure is swallowed, event count still returned`. These can mock the push subprocess while exercising the real commit path via the same temp-repo fixture used by other scenarios.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify `_recover_from_unmerged`'s new interface (e.g. always takes a `list[Path]`) and explicitly state that all call sites — including the single-event `stage_and_commit` — are updated to wrap their single path in a list, so backward-compat is achieved by call-site normalization rather than overloading.
findings.finding: `_recover_from_unmerged` is generalized to accept a full batch path list, but the plan does not specify how its interface changes or how the existing single-event callers of that function are updated/kept compatible — leaving the implementer with two diverging approaches (always-a-list vs overload).
findings.location: Scope: 'Generalize `_recover_from_unmerged` to re-add the FULL batch path list on retry' / AC: '_recover_from_unmerged` (bug 6818 UU self-heal) re-adds the full batch path list on retry'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the spec to: '`_recover_from_unmerged` is refactored to accept `paths: list[Path]`; the existing single-event `stage_and_commit` call site wraps its single path as `[placed_path]` when calling `_recover_from_unmerged`.' This makes the interface unambiguous and keeps the existing tests green without overloading.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the existing single-event `stage_and_commit` call site adapts to the generalized `_recover_from_unmerged` signature (e.g., 'the function accepts `list[str]`; the single-event call site wraps its `relative_path` in a one-element list').
findings.finding: AC #4 requires generalizing `_recover_from_unmerged` to accept the full batch path list, but the plan does not specify how the existing single-event call site (`stage_and_commit` line 161) should adapt to the new signature — leaving ambiguity about whether the single-path caller wraps its argument in a list, or whether the function gains an overloaded/union signature.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: '`_recover_from_unmerged` signature changes from `(tracker, event_relpath: str, commit_msg)` to `(tracker, relpaths: list[str], commit_msg)`; the existing single-event call site in `stage_and_commit` passes `[relative_path]`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the return value of `batch_write_and_push` in the plan (e.g., 'returns the event count, same as `batch_stage_and_commit`; push errors are logged but not raised').
findings.finding: `batch_write_and_push` return value is not stated in the plan; the single-event `write_and_push` return value is also unspecified, leaving an executor to guess whether to return the event count, None, or a push result.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC line 2: '`batch_write_and_push` variant: batch commit then ONE best-effort push'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC2: '…returns the event count (same as `batch_stage_and_commit`); push failure is logged and does not raise.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'canonical-byte parity' concretely in the plan (e.g., 'the file produced by the batch path for a given event dict is byte-for-byte identical to the file produced by the single-event path for the same dict').
findings.finding: 'Canonical-byte parity vs single path' in the test-coverage list is ambiguous — it is unclear whether it means bit-for-bit identical file bytes, identical JSON structure, or equivalent deserialized content, leaving test authors to decide what assertion to write.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC line 7: 'New unit tests … canonical-byte parity vs single path'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'canonical-byte parity vs single path' with: 'file written by batch path for event E is byte-for-byte identical to the file written by `stage_and_commit` for the same event dict (assert open(batch_path).read() == open(single_path).read())'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a cleanup path (try/finally or equivalent) for pre-lock temp files: on any exception during pre-lock staging OR on failure to acquire the lock, unlink all temp files created so far in that batch call.
findings.finding: Temp files created during the pre-lock validate+canonical-stage phase have no cleanup path if lock acquisition itself fails (e.g., raises an exception or times out). These files will accumulate on disk indefinitely.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Validate + canonical-stage every event BEFORE taking the lock'
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the pre-lock staging loop in a try block; in the finally/except, unlink all temp paths accumulated so far before re-raising. This mirrors the inside-lock rollback already specified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that the planned B2 importer story concretely requires raw `(ticket_id, event_dict)` batch writes (not higher-level command calls), and cite at least one additional planned call-site beyond B2 to meet the Rule-of-Three bar before adding `batch_stage_and_commit` to the event_append API.
findings.finding: The `batch_stage_and_commit` / `batch_write_and_push` abstraction has zero existing call-sites in the codebase and only one named planned consumer ('the importer, B2'), which is a future story, not a current done-definition. The existing importer (`import_ndjson.py`) already handles write-batching via `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off` + one final push without using `stage_and_commit` directly, suggesting the seam the new API provides may not actually be the interface the importer needs. This falls below the Rule-of-Three threshold for introducing a new abstraction.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a profiling citation or concrete scale estimate (e.g., 'N-ticket import takes Xs, X% in lock acquire') to the §Why section to justify the per-commit overhead optimization, or note this is a correctness/reentrancy fix (the non-re-entrant lock constraint) rather than a performance optimization.
findings.finding: The plan cites '~3.3 commits/event' as a performance problem motivating the batch primitive, but provides no profiling result, benchmark, or scale estimate to establish that lock-acquire count (rather than push latency, already addressed via `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off`) is the actual bottleneck. Per G-9 (scale calibration), premature-optimization findings require affirmative evidence of the performance problem.
findings.location: Plan §Why — 'commits one git graph per event (~3.3 commits/event)'
findings.suggested_fix: If the primary motivation is the lock reentrancy constraint (callers cannot loop stage_and_commit under one lock) rather than commit-count performance, reframe §Why around that correctness constraint and drop the performance framing — this avoids the premature-optimization read.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and tighten the 'lock is NOT re-entrant' claim: ground it specifically in the mkdir leg (the actual source of non-reentrancy) and note that the property is lost when `dual_window=False` after bash migration — the plan should address whether `batch_stage_and_commit` needs to remain the preferred batch API after that transition.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'The write lock (`_store/lock.py`) is NOT re-entrant, so a caller cannot loop `stage_and_commit` under one lock' as the primary motivation for the batch primitive. The actual implementation uses `fcntl.flock(LOCK_EX|LOCK_NB)` on a NEWLY opened fd (`os.open` each call) plus a `mkdir` leg. On Linux/POSIX, `flock(2)` is per-open-file-description: a second call from the same process that opens a new fd to the same file will succeed (upgrade, not block), making the fcntl leg transparently re-entrant. The mkdir leg IS effectively non-re-entrant (the stale-check in `_mkdir_lock_is_stale` tests liveness via `os.kill`, so the owning live PID would NOT be reclaimed). The net practical result is that `write_lock` IS non-re-entrant at the mkdir leg, but the plan's unqualified claim that 'The write lock is NOT re-entrant' is underpinned by only half of the implementation; the fcntl half is actually re-entrant within the same process. The plan should ground this assertion precisely against the mkdir leg to be accurate.
findings.location: Why section — 'The write lock (`_store/lock.py`) is NOT re-entrant, so a caller cannot loop `stage_and_commit` under one lock.'
findings.suggested_fix: Update the Why section to read: 'The write lock's mkdir leg (`_store/lock.py`) is NOT re-entrant (the live-owner stale-check prevents same-process reclaim), so a caller cannot safely loop `stage_and_commit` under one lock while `dual_window=True`. After bash migration (`dual_window=False`, fcntl-only), re-entrancy would technically be possible, but `batch_stage_and_commit` remains the preferred API for atomicity, not just lock-count reduction.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the unverifiable '~3.3 commits/event' metric with a grounded explanation of where extra commits arise (e.g., 'one event commit + one optional UU-recovery retry commit + one possible push-reconcile merge commit = up to 3 commits per event in the worst case'), or remove the metric and simply state 'one lock acquire and one commit per event.'
findings.finding: The plan states the single-event path commits '~3.3 commits/event'. This specific metric appears nowhere in the codebase, documentation, or test files — it is an unverifiable empirical claim. The code clearly shows exactly one `git commit` per `stage_and_commit` call, plus potentially one retry commit inside `_recover_from_unmerged` plus one merge commit in `write_and_push`'s push path under non-fast-forward conditions, but '3.3' is not derivable from reading the implementation.
findings.location: Why section — 'The single-event write path (`stage_and_commit`) commits one git graph per event (~3.3 commits/event).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete cardinality assertion to the 'N-in-one-commit' test spec, e.g., 'assert the git-commit subprocess was called exactly once (mock call count == 1) and `git log --oneline` shows exactly one new commit SHA after the batch call'.
findings.finding: The 'exactly one commit' claim (AC1: 'exactly one commit') has no cardinality-asserting proving command in the test spec — the listed test scenario 'N-in-one-commit' does not specify how the test will assert commit count, risking a fixture that passes without actually verifying the commit boundary.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC line 1 and AC line 7 test scenarios
findings.suggested_fix: In the test coverage bullet, add: '…assert `git_commit` is called exactly once (e.g., mock.call_count == 1 or `len(repo.git.log("--oneline", "-n", "2").splitlines()) == initial_count + 1`).`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State an explicit upper bound on batch size (or confirm unbounded is intentional and safe), and specify whether callers are responsible for chunking or the primitive self-chunks, given this is explicitly the bulk-import seam for B2.
findings.finding: The plan names this primitive as the seam for bulk callers (the importer, B2) but states no upper bound on `len(items)` or any load expectation. For very large batches, all temp files are staged before the lock and all paths are passed to `git add` in one invocation — command-line argument limits or memory pressure could become relevant, yet no cap or chunking strategy is specified.
findings.location: Scope section / AC: `batch_stage_and_commit(tracker, items)` — items is a sequence with no stated bound
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope or AC: 'Batch size is unbounded by this primitive; callers (B2/importer) are responsible for chunking if needed. Tested up to N=X events in CI.' Or add a `max_items` guard with a documented default.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify observability for the batch path: add structured log entries (at minimum) for validation failures, rollback actions, commit errors, and push failures; clarify whether push failure is logged-and-swallowed or surfaced to the caller.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no logging, metrics, or alerting for the new failure points introduced by the batch path (validation errors, rename failures, git-add failures, commit failures, rollback execution). Operators have no visibility into batch write failures or partial rollbacks in production.
findings.location: Scope section / AC: All-or-nothing rollback AC line
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit logging contract to the AC: e.g., 'Each rollback emits a WARNING log with ticket_id list and failure reason; push failure emits WARNING and returns without raising'. Update the AC to require at least one log assertion in the test suite (e.g., assert logger.warning called on commit failure).

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
